articles.title: Validate DNS and SRV records on the routing path
articles.summary: Bad DNS or SRV data can make the call-routing layer fail even when SIP services are healthy.
content: When to use: Calls fail to route after a domain or peer lookup and the error follows one destination realm.
Action: Verify DNS A, AAAA, and SRV records, TTL values, and any cached stale answers on the routing nodes.
Why it works: Routing defects often begin with name-resolution drift rather than a failure in the SIP application itself.
Verify: The correct next hop is selected again and route lookup latency returns to baseline.
Guardrail: Purge stale cache only after confirming the authoritative data is correct.

articles.title: Inspect initial filter criteria and service-chain routing logic
articles.summary: Misapplied iFC or service-routing policy can send traffic down the wrong IMS path.
content: When to use: Requests enter the platform but are routed to the wrong application server or service chain.
Action: Compare the active iFC or equivalent policy set with a known-good subscriber and confirm the trigger conditions are still correct.
Why it works: Routing rules are often data driven, so one misordered or overly broad policy can change the entire call path.
Verify: The expected service chain is selected again for the affected subscriber or realm.
Capture: Save the matched rule or trigger block that caused the misroute for future RCA grounding.

articles.title: Repair Route and Record-Route normalization between peers
articles.summary: Broken route headers can make otherwise valid requests loop or terminate on the wrong hop.
content: When to use: Requests loop, hit the wrong upstream, or arrive without the expected route context.
Action: Check Route and Record-Route handling across proxies, especially after topology-hiding or interconnect policy changes.
Why it works: Header normalization problems often masquerade as generic routing errors until message traces are compared hop by hop.
Verify: The request follows the intended path and loop indicators disappear from the trace.
Risk: Inconsistent header rewriting across peers can reintroduce the issue after only a partial fix.

articles.title: Verify realm and domain mappings after onboarding changes
articles.summary: Routing incidents commonly follow a bad realm or domain map update.
content: When to use: One destination domain or tenant suddenly stops routing after a policy or onboarding change.
Action: Audit domain mapping tables, peer selections, and any default route fallback used for unknown realms.
Why it works: A single wrong map entry can redirect all traffic for that domain into a dead or unauthorized path.
Verify: Requests for the affected realm match the intended route policy again and no longer use the fallback path.
Aftercare: Add a targeted route validation check for newly onboarded realms.

articles.title: Rebalance upstream trunk priorities when the preferred route is degraded
articles.summary: A degraded preferred trunk can cause widespread routing failures until priorities are adjusted.
content: When to use: The platform still has alternative trunks, but policy keeps steering traffic to the degraded preferred route.
Action: Lower the priority or temporarily disable the unhealthy route so traffic can shift to healthy peers.
Why it works: Controlled rerouting restores service without requiring a full routing-policy redesign during the incident.
Verify: Call attempts begin succeeding through the backup path and route error volume drops quickly.
Risk: Backup trunks may have capacity or cost constraints, so monitor both service success and new load.

articles.title: Restore ENUM or least-cost-routing data before touching SIP servers
articles.summary: If external route data is stale, SIP servers may be healthy but still unable to select a valid destination.
content: When to use: Route selection fails during number translation or external policy lookup rather than inside the proxy itself.
Action: Validate ENUM records, least-cost-routing tables, or any external translation dataset used by the route engine.
Why it works: Routing data defects are often upstream of the SIP application, so server restarts do not address the real cause.
Verify: The correct route candidate list repopulates and translation lookups return expected next hops.
Documentation: Note which dataset version was active so future incidents can correlate to the same external dependency.

articles.title: Audit topology-hiding rules that may strip required routing context
articles.summary: Topology-hiding changes can accidentally remove headers the next hop still needs.
content: When to use: Routing errors began after a security or topology-hiding policy change on the edge.
Action: Review which headers are removed, rewritten, or encrypted and confirm the next hop still has enough context to route correctly.
Why it works: Security-focused header handling can unintentionally break downstream routing assumptions.
Verify: Calls route normally once the required context is preserved or restored.
Guardrail: Keep the minimum necessary visibility while preserving the privacy goals of topology hiding.

articles.title: Confirm peer gateway health before concluding the route table is wrong
articles.summary: Routing may be correct while the selected peer gateway is simply unhealthy.
content: When to use: Requests are routed to the expected peer, but the peer fails to respond or rejects calls unexpectedly.
Action: Check peer gateway health, admission state, and maintenance flags before rewriting routing policy.
Why it works: Good routing data still fails if the chosen peer is unavailable, saturated, or administratively blocked.
Verify: Peer health returns or traffic is shifted to a healthy alternative and route failures decline.
Evidence: Attach the peer health snapshot to the incident for future retrieval.

articles.title: Clear stale routing cache after authoritative data has been corrected
articles.summary: Old route cache entries can keep misrouting traffic after the source of truth is fixed.
content: When to use: The routing dataset is fixed, but requests continue to follow the previous bad route on one or more nodes.
Action: Refresh or invalidate route caches in a controlled order so all nodes consume the corrected data.
Why it works: Routing cache lag creates a long tail of failures even after the original issue is resolved.
Verify: Nodes converge on the same route decision and the old bad next hop disappears from new traces.
Risk: Cache invalidation can spike upstream lookups, so stagger the operation if the dataset backend is fragile.

articles.title: Rollback the recent routing-policy change when blast radius is immediate
articles.summary: Broad route failures right after a policy rollout usually justify a fast rollback.
content: When to use: Routing errors begin immediately after a policy or route-table rollout and affect multiple destinations.
Action: Restore the prior policy revision, confirm route selection normalizes, and capture the failing diff for later analysis.
Why it works: Rollback stabilizes the core call path quickly and reduces speculation during a live outage.
Verify: Success rate returns on the previous revision and new routing failures stop appearing.
Aftercare: Recreate the failure in a test environment with the captured diff before reattempting deployment.
